title: Matos means business: ANZ’s new bonus shake-up
full_text:
If there was any doubt that ANZ boss Nuno Matos is serious about rewiring the bank’s culture, the past few weeks should have settled it. Matos is now directly linking bonus payments to the four corporate values he quietly introduced last month in a move that puts financial clout behind principles that tend to gather dust at most corporates.
The changes, unveiled to staff this week, are expected to impact thousands of managers classified internally as Group 3 or higher.
This is the bank’s middle and senior management tier – precisely the layer of an organisation that can make or break cultural change.
Significantly, Matos is also considering rolling out a similar shake-up of bonuses that will impact a much broader pool of management or supervisor staff (internally classed as Group 4 or lower) but this could require negotiation with unions and is likely to be another year away.
Under the changes to that apply from this financial year, senior managers will have their final pay determined by a new five-point performance rating. This is designed to draw a clearer line between individual performance, behaviour, reward, and bank-wide performance.
Under the move, the managers will now see separate bonuses paid for individual “at risk” pay and “group” performance, combined into a single performance reward. Under the old model, a strong individual result could cushion the blow of a poor bank-wide outcome.
The bank’s head of HR Elisa Clements told staff in a memo the changes support ANZ’s ambitions to become a high-performing organisation where “its clear to understand how performance, behaviour and reward connect”.
The changes “respond to the need for better customers outcomes, stronger risk management and greater consistency in how performance, behaviour and reward decisions are made,” Clements says in the memo seen by The Australian.
The Australian revealed last month Matos had introduced a set of corporate values which have a harder edge and based around an execution culture as well as accountability. The four values are: put customers first; deliver with excellence; own the outcome, and work as one. The pay shake-up is clearly a tangible step to putting the values in practice.
The move is a further insight into how Matos plans to shift the dial not only on the financial performance of ANZ, but also its culture - including non-financial risk.
When he joined ANZ a year ago, the lender was on the nose after repeated classes with regulators. This culminated with the bank agreeing to pay a fine of up to $240m to resolve a number of outstanding legal actions with ASIC, including acting unconscionably around bond trading deals.
Matos, a former senior HSBC executive who has spent his career navigating large, complex financial institutions, speaks regularly about the need for simplicity, for execution discipline, and for genuine accountability at every level of the organisation.
The bonus redesign is best understood as the carrot in a broader transformation strategy. The stick, is the axing of approximately 4,500 full-time roles and contractor positions as Matos moves aggressively to reduce the bank’s cost base. That program is around 80 per cent complete.
Matos also sent a signal to staff last year around accountability by forfeiting his own million dollar bonus payments even though the bank’s string of regulatory issues occurred before he arrived 12 months ago.
Whether the changes ultimately shift ANZ’s dial on culture – alongside its financial performance and non-financial risk profile – remains to be seen. Cultural transformation is the hardest for big banks, and needs to be played as a long game,
johnstone@theaustralian.com.au
Nationwide News Pty Ltd.
Document AUSTOL0020260514em5e0028l
summary: ANZ CEO Nuno Matos is changing how bonuses are awarded, linking them to new corporate values to improve the bank's culture and performance. This affects thousands of managers and aims to create clearer connections between individual and group performance.
author: Eric Johnston
heading: Banking and Economy
key_points: Bonus changes apply to Group 3 managers and above.
New five-point performance rating system introduced.
Separate bonuses for individual and group performance combined.
Matos introduced four corporate values focused on accountability.
Approximately 4,500 jobs cut to reduce costs.
ticker_line: ANZ links bonuses to new corporate values
